inclusion_criteria: Children aged 10–13 years in the mixed dentition with clinically and/or radiographically diagnosed palatally displaced maxillary permanent canines (PDC).
Intervention is extraction of the corresponding maxillary primary canine(s) as interceptive treatment; comparator is no interceptive treatment (observation/waitlist) or delayed treatment.

Report eruption-related outcomes, such as successful spontaneous eruption or normalization/improvement of eruptive position/trajectory (clinical or radiographic), with defined follow-up sufficient to assess eruption.
Comparative human studies: randomized controlled trials, controlled clinical trials, or prospective/retrospective cohort studies with a comparison group.
exclusion_criteria: Population outside scope: not palatally displaced maxillary canines, outside 10–13 years, or not in mixed dentition; syndromic/craniofacial anomalies (e.g., cleft lip/palate).
Extraction combined with concurrent active orthodontic or surgical interventions at baseline (e.g., orthodontic traction, rapid maxillary expansion, headgear, surgical exposure).
Non-comparative or non-original research: case reports/series without comparator, reviews, editorials, conference abstracts, in vitro or animal studies.
Do not report eruption/eruptive position outcomes or provide insufficient data to determine normalization/successful eruption.
screening_criteria.description: Screening criteria to identify comparative studies evaluating extraction of primary canines versus observation/delayed treatment in 10–13-year-old mixed-dentition patients with palatally displaced maxillary canines, measuring normalization of position and successful eruption.

included_articles.title: Two interceptive approaches to palatally displaced canines: a prospective longitudinal study.
Effect of interceptive extraction of deciduous canine on palatally displaced maxillary canine: a prospective randomized controlled study.
A randomized clinical study of two interceptive approaches to palatally displaced canines.
Extraction of the deciduous canine as an interceptive treatment in children with palatally displaced canines - part II: possible predictors of success and cut-off points for a spontaneous eruption.
Extraction of the deciduous canine as an interceptive treatment in children with palatal displaced canines - part I: shall we extract the deciduous canine or not?
The use of panoramic radiographs to decide when interceptive extraction is beneficial in children with palatally displaced canines based on a randomized clinical trial.
